items.title: Pair sigmoid with BCE
items.avoid: Training with MSE on 0/1 labels or applying BCE to unbounded linear scores without sigmoid.
items.prefer: Model ŷ = σ(wᵀx + b) and minimize BCE; in PyTorch use BCEWithLogitsLoss on raw logits.
items.why: This matches Bernoulli maximum likelihood and keeps predictions in valid probability range.
items.example: nn.Linear(d, 1) + BCEWithLogitsLoss — not MSELoss on float labels.

items.title: Scale features before training
items.avoid: Fitting on raw features with wildly different scales (income in dollars vs age in years).
items.prefer: Standardize or normalize continuous columns; one-hot encode categoricals consistently.
items.why: Gradient descent converges faster and coefficient magnitudes become comparable for interpretation.
items.example: Use StandardScaler on numeric columns before LogisticRegression when not using regularized closed-form solvers.

items.title: Evaluate probabilities, not just labels
items.avoid: Reporting only accuracy at threshold 0.5 on imbalanced validation data.
items.prefer: Track log loss (BCE), AUC, and precision-recall; tune threshold for the deployment cost profile.
items.why: Logistic regression is chosen for probabilistic outputs — discard that value and you lose the main benefit.
items.example: Report log_loss and ROC-AUC on validation; pick threshold where recall ≥ 0.85 for fraud.

items.title: Know when to add nonlinearity
items.avoid: Forcing plain logistic regression when validation AUC plateaus and EDA shows curved class separation.
items.prefer: Add interaction/polynomial features, try gradient-boosted trees, or upgrade to a neural binary head.
items.why: Logistic regression is a linear boundary in feature space — it cannot invent curvature on its own.
items.example: If two features separate classes only when x1*x2 is large, add that product feature or switch model family.
